events.description: Last sixty patents issued as result of Declaration of Policy; records show 20 promptly mortgaged, 16 sold outright, 24 clear but likely to be encumbered
Charles W. Edmister trip among Shawnees to investigate patent applications, encountered loan agent inquiring about Billy Hood
Competency commission hearings for Indians applying for patents; those asking found less qualified than those who did not ask
Act of Congress approved providing allotments for Indians
Family had team taken away under reimbursable plan due to mortgage default
Sixteen tracts sold outright by patentees of the sixty reviewed
Twenty of sixty patented tracts promptly mortgaged, only one released
Over five hundred Indians released from Government control and granted citizenship

financial_transactions.description: Twenty of last sixty patented tracts mortgaged under Declaration of Policy, only one released
Sixteen tracts sold outright by patentees of the sixty reviewed
Attempt to negotiate loan on Billy Hood's land; application initially turned down by Superintendent
Family mortgaged team under reimbursable plan, later taken away for non-payment
Indians squandering money on peyote societies, clans, and fast living after receiving patent proceeds

relationships.subject: Charles W. Edmister
relationships.context: Farmer with the Service at Shawnee, Oklahoma

relationships.subject: Samuel Charger
relationships.context: Sioux Indian from La Plant, South Dakota

relationships.subject: Billy Hood
relationships.context: Allotted as William Panther, known as Billy Hood

fee_patents.mortgage: 20 of 60 tracts mortgaged, only one released
fee_patents.context: Last sixty patents under Declaration of Policy reviewed by Charles W. Edmister
Application made for SW 1/4 Sec 14 T28 R25; initially turned down by Superintendent; loan agent inquired about timing
Applied for patent, considered less worthy than Billy Hood by loan agent
Released from Government control on reservation; few made good, many squandered money

correspondence.subject: Condition of patent-in-fee Indians
correspondence.action_requested: Review enclosed letters regarding fee patents

correspondence.subject: Releasing Indians from Government Supervision
correspondence.outcome: Four letters enclosed from Archerd, Daniel, Phillips, Mills

correspondence.subject: Advisability of granting patents-in-fee
correspondence.action_requested: Views and suggestions concerning issuance of patents
correspondence.outcome: Generated responses from field clerks and farmers

correspondence.subject: Fee patents, restrictions, and Indian competency
correspondence.action_requested: Suggested 40-acre inalienable homestead and longer trust periods

correspondence.subject: Reply to April 2, 1921 letter on fee patents
correspondence.action_requested: Opposed indiscriminate release; advocated education-based competency

correspondence.subject: Opposition to granting fee patents and bulk citizenship
correspondence.action_requested: Stop granting patents and citizenship in bulk; maintain Federal guidance

legislative_actions.outcome: Proposed legislation to make 40 acres inalienable during life of allottee with taxes chargeable to other property
Advocated citizenship for all ex-service Indian soldiers
